spots.name: King-high dry: value vs bluff-catchers
spots.description: A range-advantage spot: OOP's range is almost all strong made hands, IP holds mostly bluff-catchers.

spots.name: Polarized vs bluff-catcher
spots.description: The classic toy game: with one pot-sized bet, value bets and bluffs must come in a 2:1 ratio and the defender calls half the time. Watch the frequencies match the theory.

spots.name: Ace-high dry: top pair battles
spots.description: Both players hold top pairs of different strength, but IP's range hides sets of kings and deuces. Kicker quality decides who bets thin and who bluff-catches.

spots.name: Paired board: pressure on bluff-catchers
spots.description: Trips and overpairs attack the paired board, but IP slow-played some queens too. Middle pairs face tough calls.

spots.name: Flush board: nut advantage
spots.description: Three spades on board. Who holds the bigger flushes dictates who can bet big and who must check-call.

spots.name: Straight completes on the river
spots.description: The ten-nine-eight runout puts straights in both ranges. Sets and overpairs are downgraded to bluff-catchers.

spots.name: Missed flush draws: who bluffs?
spots.description: The river bricks the heart draw. Busted draws make the cheapest bluffs because they never win at showdown.

spots.name: Low board: overpairs out of position
spots.description: Overpairs and second pairs navigate a low, disconnected board where thin value bets go a long way.

spots.name: Thin value with top pair
spots.description: How thin can top pair bet for value when worse pairs make up most of the calling range?

spots.name: Board pairs on the river
spots.description: The river pairs the eight, turning some two pairs into trips-plus and freezing medium-strength hands.

spots.name: Three-bet pot: overpairs vs sets
spots.description: A preflop three-bettor's overpair range meets a caller whose range hides flopped sets on a ragged board.

spots.name: Overbet with the nut advantage
spots.description: Only the OOP range contains aces-up and sets, unlocking an overbet sizing that maximizes pressure on capped hands.

spots.name: Small bet on a static board
spots.description: On a board where equities barely move, a one-third pot bet attacks weak pairs cheaply and high frequency.

spots.name: Two pair vs straight threats
spots.description: Two pair and sets want value, but queen-nine and nine-seven straights lurk in both ranges.

spots.name: Monotone flop, brick runout
spots.description: Flushes made on the flop slow-play or build the pot; bare top pairs decide how much heat they can stand.
